title: Candied orange peel
subtitle: Triple-blanched peel simmered in syrup, dried, and rolled in sugar
excerpt: Candied orange peel requires three rounds of blanching to remove the bitterness, followed by a slow simmer in a sugar syrup until the peel is translucent and the sugar has penetrated. Dried on a rack and rolled in caster sugar. Good for eating as a sweet, dipping in chocolate, or using in Christmas cake and panettone.
sourceNotes: Candied or crystallised citrus peel is documented in European domestic cookbooks from the medieval period, where preserved sweetmeats made from peel cooked in honey or sugar syrup were a standard preparation. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, includes a recipe for candied lemon and orange peel using a sugar syrup method. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932), records similar preparations in the British domestic tradition. The method of repeated blanching to remove bitterness followed by a slow sugar-syrup soak is the standard technique across all English-language domestic confectionery references.

recipe.batchNotes: Scale with additional oranges and adjust syrup proportionally. Keep the 2:1 sugar-to-water ratio.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps in a sealed jar for up to 1 month. Dip in chocolate and allow to set for a confectionery version.

glossaryTerms.definition: Orange and lemon peel contains bitter compounds (limonin and naringin) concentrated in the white pith. Blanching in fresh boiling water, draining, and repeating removes a significant proportion of these compounds with each cycle. The first blanch removes the most bitterness; the second and third remove progressively less. Three blanches is the standard for a pleasant, mild result. The peel should taste slightly bitter but not harsh after three blanches; if still very bitter, blanch a fourth time. Use fresh cold water for each blanch.

content.text: Candied peel from scratch tastes entirely different from the commercial diced kind. The strips are moist, intensely flavoured, and have a translucent amber quality when held to the light. The process is unhurried; the total active time is under 30 minutes.
Quarter the {{orange}}s and remove the flesh. Cut the peel into strips about 1 cm wide.
three times: bring a pan of water to the boil, add the peel, boil for 5 minutes, drain, and return to fresh cold water. Repeat twice more with fresh boiling water each time.

Combine the {{granulated-sugar}} and {{water}} in a medium saucepan. Stir over low heat until the sugar dissolves, then add the blanched peel. Simmer very gently for 45 minutes until the peel is translucent and the syrup has reduced to a thick glaze. Do not boil hard; the peel needs time for the sugar to penetrate.

Lift the peel out onto a cooling rack with a fork. Leave to dry at room temperature for at least 6 hours (overnight is better). Roll each piece in {{caster-sugar}} and store in a sealed jar. The peel keeps for 1 month.
